---
date: "2026-08-03"
---

## Your Data, Reproducibly: The Repository Is the Deliverable

**Learning objectives.** By the end you can:

- Lay out a research repository from a template
- Pin a conda environment that rebuilds anywhere
- Verify `.gitignore` blocks licensed data and secrets
- File the pre-analysis plan as a tagged commit

::: notes
Welcome to Week Seven, the Lab, part one of eight. This is where your project stops being a pile of notebooks on your laptop and becomes a research repository — a single, version-controlled folder a stranger could clone and understand. Over these eight parts we build that repository step by step: a sensible layout, a pinned environment, a README and data cards, a git-ignore that makes leaks impossible, and a pre-analysis plan tagged with an immutable timestamp. This opening part frames why the repository itself is your capstone deliverable.
:::

## Where Week Seven Has Been Pointing

- Everything Week Seven built points to this lab
- Chapter seven-point-two: pull data the same way every time
- Chapter seven-point-three: date your pre-analysis plan
- This lab builds the thing those chapters described
- The repository you stand up today is your capstone

::: notes
Everything Week Seven has built points here. Chapter seven-point-two taught you to pull data so the same command, run a year from now, gives the same answer — pinned, cached, logged. Chapter seven-point-three taught you to write a pre-analysis plan, and insisted it only constrains you if you can date it, and named the mechanism: a tagged Git commit. This lab is where you actually build the thing those chapters described, and it is not a throwaway exercise. The repository you stand up today is your capstone deliverable. Weeks Seven and Eight both live inside it — the dataset build, the identification memo, the first-look regressions, and next week's full analysis all commit into this same repo. Get the scaffolding right now, and the rest of the camp is building on rock.
:::

## Reproducibility Is a Property of the Repository

- A skeptic treats an unrepeatable analysis as an assertion
- Reproducibility lives in the repository, not one script
- The "result" here is operational, not statistical
- Can a fresh clone reconstruct the environment?
- Did the licensed bytes and secrets stay home?

::: notes
The discipline this lab drills is the one a referee, a replication team, or future-you cares about most: reproducibility is a property of the repository, not of any single script. A brilliant analysis whose data pull cannot be re-run, whose environment cannot be reconstructed, or whose pre-registration cannot be dated is, to a skeptic, just an assertion. We are going to make your project the opposite of an assertion. There is no synthetic-data simulation here and no estimator to recover. The result you are checking is operational: can a fresh clone of your repo, on a machine that has never seen your project, reconstruct the environment, read what the project does, and verify that the licensed bytes and the secrets never left your control? That is a yes-or-no you can actually test.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A result a stranger cannot reconstruct is not yet a result. This lab makes your project the opposite of an assertion: reproducibility becomes a property of the **repository** — and that repository is your capstone deliverable. Next: laying out the project from a sensible template.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: this lab turns your project into a research repository, and that repository is your capstone deliverable — Weeks Seven and Eight both live inside it. The key idea is that reproducibility is a property of the repository, not of any single script, and the test is operational: can a fresh clone rebuild everything while the licensed data and secrets stay home? In part two we start building, laying out the project from a sensible template.
:::
